title: Demand and the determinants of demand
description: Practice what you've learned about demand, quantity demanded, the determinants of demand in this exercise.
topic_path: Demand and the determinants of demand

assessment_items.question_content:
**All of the following will result in a shift in a demand curve except:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A change in the income of buyers
A change in the supply of a good
A change in expected future prices
A change in the price of related goods
A change in buyers' preferences

assessment_items.question_content:
*Something has changed about how consumers buy hats which resulted in the change shown in this graph.*

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following changes could this graph represent?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The figure presents a graph of two demand curves in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. One curve is labeled D 1 and starts at the vertical axis above the origin at coordinates 9 and 0 and moves downward and to the right and ends at coordinates 3 and 0. The second curve is labeled D 2 and is directly below and parallel to the first curve and starts at coordinates 0 and 6 and moves down and to the right and ends at coordinates 2 and 0. There is an arrow pointing from the first curve to the second curve.
options.caption: $\text{Figure }1$: The demand for hats

choices.content: People do not like hats as much as they did before, so their demand has decreased
People do not like hats as much as they did before, resulting in a movement along the demand curve.
People like hats more than they did before, and their demand for hats has increased
There has been a decrease in the price of hats, resulting in an increase in the quantity demanded for hats
People do not like hats as much as they did before, resulting in an increase in the quantity demanded for hats

assessment_items.question_content:
Something has changed about how consumers buy hats, which has resulted in the change shown in the graph shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following changes could this graph represent?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The figure presents a graph of two demand curves in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. One curve is labeled D 1  and starts at the vertical axis above the origin and moves downward and to the right ending slightly before it hits the horizontal axis. The second curve is labeled D 2 and is directly below and parallel to the first curve. There is an arrow pointing from the first curve to the second curve.
options.caption: A graph with two demand curves

choices.content: People do not like hats as much as they did before, so their demand has decreased
People do not like hats as much as they did before, resulting in a movement along the demand curve.
People like hats more than they did before, and their demand for hats has increased because of this
There has been a decrease in the price of hats, resulting in an increase in the quantity demanded for hats

assessment_items.question_content:
Penny thinks that the price of books will decrease next week. 

**If everything else is equal, which of the following would reflect how Penny's behavior will change in response to her expectations?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Penny will move to a new, higher quantity on her demand curve
Penny will move to a new, lower quantity along her demand curve
Penny's demand for books today will increase
Penny's demand for books today will decrease
Penny will demand more complementary goods to books

assessment_items.question_content:
Economists have observed that when average incomes increase, purchases of fast food tends to decline. 

**Based on this information, what can we definitely say about fast food?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Demand for fast food is upward sloping
Fast food has no substitutes
The price of a complement to fast food has risen
Fast food is an inferior good
Fast food is a normal good

assessment_items.question_content:
The demand for Good $X$ is given in the graph shown here.

**Which of the following could cause the shift of demand shown here?**

[[☃ image 1]]

 [[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The figure presents a graph of two demand curves in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. One curve is labeled D 1 and starts at the vertical axis above the origin at coordinates 0 and 9 and moves downward and to the right and ends at coordinates 0 and 9. The second curve is labeled D 2 and is directly above and parallel to the first curve and starts at coordinates 0 and 12 and moves down and to the right and ends at coordinates 12 and 0. There is an arrow pointing from the first curve to the second curve.
options.caption: The demand for Good $X$

choices.content: An increase in the price of good $Y$, which is a complement to good $X$
An increase in income if good $X$ is an inferior good
An increase in the number of buyers of a good
Buyers of good $X$ expect the price of good $X$ to decrease in the future
People do not like good $X$ as much as they did before

The demand for Good $X$ is given in the graph shown here.

[[☃ image 1]]

 [[☃ radio 1]]

The second curve is labeled D 2 and is directly above and parallel to the first curve. There is an arrow pointing from the first curve to the second curve.

choices.content: An increase in the price of good $Y$ which is a complement to good $X$
An increase in income if good $X$ is an inferior good

assessment_items.question_content:
Pam considers t-shirts and gym classes to be complementary goods. 

**How will Pam react if the price of gym classes increases?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Her quantity demanded of gym classes will decrease, and her demand for t-shirts will decrease.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes will increase, and her demand for t-shirts will decrease.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes will decrease, and her demand for t-shirts will be unaffected.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes will increase, and her demand for t-shirts will increase.
Her quantity demanded of gym classes won’t change, and her demand for t-shirts will increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
Meeps and Blops are two goods that are related to each other.  When the price of Meeps goes down, the demand for Blops goes down.

**Based only on the information given here, what kind of goods are Meeps and Blops?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: complements
substitutes
inferior goods
normal goods
superior goods

assessment_items.question_content:
As a result of an increase in consumer incomes, the demand for Meeps has decreased. 

**Based on this information, what can we definitely say about what type of good a Meep is?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It is a normal good.
It is an inferior good.
It is a substitute good.
It is a complement good.
It has many substitutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following movements would represent an increase in the demand for a good?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
The figure shows a graph of two demand curves in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. The first curve is labeled D 1 and starts at coordinates 0 and 9 and ends at the coordinates 9 and 0. There are two points on the first curve. The first point is labeled S at coordinates 2 and 7. The second point is labeled U and is at coordinates 6 and 3. 

The second curve is labeled D 2 and lies above and parallel to the first curve. The second curve starts at coordinates 0 and 12 and ends at coordinates 12 and 0. There are two points on the second curve. The first point is labeled R at coordinates 5 and 7. The second point is labeled T and is at coordinates 9 and 3.

choices.content: From $S$ to $R$
From $S$ to $U$
From $T$ to $U$
From $R$ to $T$
From $R$ to $S$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the two following movements in this graph would represent an increase in quantity demanded but not a change in demand?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt:
The figure shows a graph of two demand curves in the first quadrant of the cartesian plane. The horizontal axis is labeled Q and the vertical axis is labeled P. The first curve is labeled D 1 and starts at coordinates 0 and 9 and ends at the coordinates 9 and 0. There are two points on the first curve. The first point is labeled S at coordinates 2 and 7. The second point is labeled U and is at coordinates 6 and 3. 

The second curve is labeled D 2 and lies above and parallel to the first curve. The second curve starts at coordinates 0 and 12 and ends at coordinates 12 and 0. There are two points on the second curve. The first point is labeled R at coordinates 5 and 7. The second point is labeled T and is at coordinates 9 and 3.
options.caption: The demand for Good $X$

choices.content: From point $S$ to point $R$
From point $U$ to point $T$
From point $R$ to point $U$
From point $S$ to point $U$
From point $S$ to point $T$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes a change in quantity demanded and a change in demand?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: $\Delta Q_d$ is a movement along a curve and $\Delta D$ is a movement of the entire curve.
Both $\Delta Q_d$ and $\Delta D$ occur when price changes.
$\Delta D$ and $\Delta Qd$ both change in response to income, expectations, preferences, number of buyers, and prices of other goods.
The two terms can be used interchangeably.
$\Delta D$ occurs when the cost of inputs to production increase, $\Delta Q_d$ occurs when the cost of inputs to production decrease

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following correctly describes a change in quantity demanded and a change in demand?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: A change in quantity demanded is a response to a price change and a change in demand is a response to a non-price change
A change in demand and a change in quantity demanded both happen in response to price changes
Both describe a movement along a single demand curve
The two terms can be used interchangeably
A change in quantity demanded occurs when price increases and a change in demand occur when a price decreases
